title: Nicomachean Ethics
subtitle: Ἠθικὰ Νικομάχεια — An Inquiry into the Good Life
author: Aristotle

recommendedTranslation.title: Aristotle: Nicomachean Ethics
recommendedTranslation.note: The standard scholarly translation; precise, philosophically rich, with thorough notes and a glossary.

alternativeTranslations.translator: J. A. K. Thomson (revised by Hugh Tredennick)
alternativeTranslations.note: The classic readable Penguin edition; first choice for general readers.
The influential 1925 literal translation that shaped a century of Anglophone philosophy.
Concise, accurate, and accessible; ideal for students.
A recent scholarly translation with a useful introduction and notes.

themes: The Role of Habit in Character Formation
awards: Foundational text of Western virtue ethics
One of the most influential books in the history of philosophy
Has never gone out of print since the first printed edition (1477)
excerpt: Every art and every inquiry, and similarly every action and pursuit, is thought to aim at some good; and for this reason the good has rightly been declared to be that at which all things aim. But a certain difference is found among ends; some are activities, others are products apart from the activities themselves. Where there are ends apart from the actions, the products are by nature better than the activities. Since there are many actions, arts, and sciences, the ends turn out to be many as well; for health is the end of medicine, a ship of shipbuilding, victory of strategy, and wealth of housecraft. But as many of the arts as belong to one capacity — for example, bridle-making and every other art that makes equipment for horsemanship — fall under horsemanship, and similarly all the arts that make equipment for a given activity fall under the activity they equip. In all these cases, the ends of the master arts are to be preferred to the ends of the subordinate arts, since the latter are pursued for the sake of the former.
